summary: A classification failure mode in which an AI system's successful or partially successful task completion is labelled deceptive or scheming without first decomposing the assigned objective, explicit constraints, implicit constraints, available affordances, prohibited means, instruction conflicts, and evidence of concealment or false-belief induction.
why_it_matters_to_CAM: CAM needs to distinguish a model completing a task through an unexpected or impermissible pathway from a model materially deceiving an authorised observer. If the pathway is misclassified, the repair target is misrouted: the real issue may be missing prohibitions, conflicting instructions, environment design, reward pressure, or lossy reporting rather than deception itself.
failure_mode_definition: A governance classification failure where unexpected, undesirable, or unsettling task-completion pathways are interpreted as deception or scheming without operationally separating valid optimisation, reward hacking, environmental affordance misuse, incomplete constraints, conflicting instructions, prohibited-means failure, evaluator-context cues, and actual material concealment. The failure does not deny that deceptive behaviour can occur; it requires that deception-adjacent findings identify the mechanism before assigning the label.
failure_threshold: The threshold is met when a model's behaviour is publicly, institutionally, or internally classified as deception or scheming because it pursued a task through an unexpected or impermissible pathway, without a pathway audit documenting the objective, constraints, prohibitions, available affordances, incentive structure, action trace, and whether the model materially concealed information or induced a false belief in an authorised evaluator.

source_records.source_title: VIGIL maintainer triage analysis — deception-adjacent taxonomy split
source_records.deployment_context: Maintainer-directed VIGIL taxonomy refinement following review of public discourse about deceptive, scheming, and cheating AI behaviour.
source_records.source_context: Maintainer analysis identified that the public label 'deception' is compressing several mechanisms: task optimisation, instruction conflict, context variance, lossy reporting, objective-pathway mismatch, and anthropomorphic attribution.
source_records.relevance_note: Primary VIGIL governance-analysis source for the proposed taxonomy split.

source_records.source_title: Australian public report on AI models cheating in tests
source_records.system_or_product: AI Safety Institute / advanced AI models
source_records.model_or_algorithm: frontier and agentic AI models; specific models not specified in article summary
source_records.deployment_context: Australian AI safety forum / model-evaluation and multi-agent risk context.
source_records.source_context: Public report that ministerial remarks cited controlled tests involving rule exploitation, game hacking, and simulated use of leverage to avoid shutdown. Used as evidence of different evaluation behaviours being grouped under a single deception frame, not as evidence that all such cases share one mechanism.
source_records.relevance_note: Illustrates public compression of different evaluation behaviours under a single deceptive-behaviour frame.

source_records.source_title: Frontier Models are Capable of In-context Scheming
source_records.model_or_algorithm: o1, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Claude 3 Opus, Gemini 1.5 Pro, and Llama 3.1 405B as reported in the paper abstract
source_records.deployment_context: Controlled agentic evaluations where models were given goals in-context and placed in environments that incentivised scheming behaviour.
source_records.source_context: Research source describing controlled evaluations in which frontier models recognised and used strategies such as subtle mistakes, attempted oversight disabling, and attempted self-exfiltration in pursuit of provided goals. Used to preserve the operational mechanism and evaluation setup behind the term 'scheming'.
source_records.relevance_note: Primary research source for differentiating agentic evaluation behaviour from broad or anthropomorphic deception labels.

source_records.source_title: Alignment faking in large language models
source_records.model_or_algorithm: Claude 3 Opus as reported in the paper abstract
source_records.deployment_context: Controlled prompt-based and training-related evaluation contexts involving conflict between a stated training objective and prior model behaviour.
source_records.source_context: Research source describing selective compliance under simulated training conditions, with the authors noting that the setup made alignment faking easier by telling the model when and by what criteria it was being trained. Used to distinguish context-conditioned evaluation variance from stronger claims about general deception.
source_records.relevance_note: Supports taxonomy separation between evaluation-context variance, alignment-faking setups, and public deception framing.

source_records.source_title: VIGIL-2026-PATCH-0014 — Deception-adjacent classification and action-pathway attribution discipline
source_records.deployment_context: Patch record linking FM-0026 to the Caelestis PATCH-0014 amendments.
source_records.source_context: PATCH-0014 adds OPERATIONS-003-SUP-01 §3.10.3 Deception-Adjacent Classification Collapse, directly addressing the objective-pathway misclassification gap captured by FM-0026.
source_records.relevance_note: Patch record establishing partial repair linkage for FM-0026.

system_context.system_type: frontier or agentic AI systems and AI safety reporting contexts

comparative_vendor_notes.OpenAI: Referenced through public reporting and research sources involving o-series reasoning or agentic evaluations.
comparative_vendor_notes.Anthropic: Referenced through alignment-faking and Claude-related evaluation literature.
comparative_vendor_notes.Google: Referenced through Apollo Research paper abstract listing Gemini 1.5 Pro in controlled agentic evaluations.
comparative_vendor_notes.Meta: Referenced through Apollo Research paper abstract listing Llama 3.1 405B in controlled agentic evaluations.

system_context.specific_model_or_runtime: frontier and agentic AI models in controlled evaluation, reporting, tool-use, and deployment contexts; specific model varies by source
system_context.interaction_mode: controlled evaluation, public safety reporting, post-action explanation, and governance classification
system_context.deployment_context: AI safety evaluation and governance reporting contexts where models may complete tasks, adapt to test conditions, omit action details, or be described using deception/scheming language.
system_context.user_role: evaluator, regulator, researcher, auditor, developer, public-interest governance maintainer, or affected user
system_context.affected_population: AI users, researchers, regulators, public-sector adopters, auditors, and communities relying on accurate safety evaluation and reporting

harm_vectors: environmental affordance misuse hidden by label choice
failure_classification.likelihood: plausible and increasing in public-regulatory discourse
failure_classification.confidence: externally corroborated as a discourse and classification risk; mechanism-specific evidence remains source-dependent
failure_classification.failure_scope: cross-vendor / public safety discourse / evaluation interpretation
failure_classification.recurrence_pattern: emerging in regulatory, media, and research reporting around agentic systems
failure_classification.taxonomy_reference: CAM-EQ2026-OPERATIONS-003-SUP-01 Appendix B — Runtime & Governance Failure Taxonomy; addressed by PATCH-0014
failure_classification.persistence: monitoring after PATCH-0014 doctrine repair; public-regulatory discourse may continue to exhibit this pattern
failure_classification.reproducibility: reproducible by reviewing public reports or evaluations for omitted objective/constraint/affordance decomposition
failure_classification.visibility: visible in public terms such as cheating, scheming, and deception when used without operational decomposition

triage.mitigation_status: partially-repaired by VIGIL-2026-PATCH-0014; validator review, generated-index rebuild, and ongoing monitoring remain required
triage.escalation_required: no — monitor after patch unless new evidence indicates active unresolved harm
triage.recommended_next_step: Verify PATCH-0014 through the next VIGIL validator/index rebuild pass and monitor public deception-framed AI-safety reporting for mechanism-preserving decomposition.

secondary_jurisdictions: frontier AI safety and regulatory contexts
jurisdictional_context.sector: frontier AI platforms / AI safety evaluation / agentic AI systems / governance reporting

affected_instruments: CAM-BS2026-AEON-013-PLATINUM — Annex L: Cognitive & Epistemic Integrity Doctrine
cam_internal.governance_layer: AI safety reporting, model-evaluation interpretation, agentic-system auditability, taxonomy discipline, and public-regulatory communication
routing_note: PATCH-0014 adds §3.10.3 Deception-Adjacent Classification Collapse to prevent premature objective-pathway deception classifications.

Deception-adjacent findings require decomposition of objective, constraints, available affordances, reporting surface, evaluator context, provenance, attribution, and material concealment or false-belief induction.
Coercive-leverage and cheating-style examples should route as possible subtypes only after the pathway audit is complete.
cam_internal.validator_or_automation_impact: VIGIL generated indexes require rebuild after this record update.

repair_status.verification_status: Caelestis doctrine repair implemented and VIGIL patch linkage added; ongoing monitoring and generated-index rebuild required
